sources.notes: Read the full statement, output requirement, and constraints: n <= 100, sum of n^3 <= 10^6, and at most n^2 swaps may be output.

Official tutorial and C++ code. Confirmed the Manhattan perfect-matching lower bound, the two coordinate-uncrossing phases, the existence condition a_i <= x_j < x_i <= a_j, the O(n^4) min-cost-flow implementation, and the optional O(n^3) KM optimization.
Consulted the first full Luogu solution and implementation; it independently presents the point model, minimum-cost perfect matching, and horizontal-then-vertical constructive realization.
Used as an independent proof cross-check, especially the pigeonhole argument guaranteeing a partner whose current and target coordinates straddle the selected point.

Re-derived the factor-1/2 lower bound and both uncrossing phases. A brute-force checker tested 1,200 random pairs of permutations for 2 <= n <= 7 against all target matchings; final permutation, cost equality, partner existence, and the n^2 operation bound all held.

annotation.summary: Given permutations p and q, output a sequence of swaps changing p into q with minimum total cost, where swapping positions i and j costs min(|i-j|, |p_i-p_j|).
annotation.constraints: There are up to 10^4 tests, 2 <= n <= 100, and the sum of n^3 over tests is at most 10^6. The construction must output at most n^2 swaps, so polynomial assignment plus an explicit uncrossing sequence is intended.
annotation.core_idea: Regard each current element as a labeled point (i,p_i). A physical swap can be interpreted either as swapping the two labels' x-coordinates for cost |i-j| or as swapping their y-coordinates for cost |p_i-p_j|; choosing the cheaper interpretation gives exactly the original cost. Relax the requirement that two labels move together. If initial point i is assigned to target point (a_i,q_{a_i}), independent movement costs one half of its Manhattan distance, so every valid swap sequence is bounded below by one half of the minimum perfect-matching cost sum_i(|i-a_i|+|p_i-q_{a_i}|). For any minimum matching this bound is attainable. First fix all x-coordinates. Among mismatched labels choose i with minimum target a_i; necessarily a_i < x_i. A pigeonhole count guarantees a label j with a_i <= x_j < x_i <= a_j. Swapping x_i and x_j moves both labels toward their targets, decreases total x-distance by 2|x_i-x_j|, and is charged |x_i-x_j|. Repeat, then apply the identical argument to y_i versus target q_{a_i}. These coordinate swaps are actual swaps of the entries at the labels' current x-positions. Their charged total equals half the matching objective; actual swap cost is no larger, while the matching relaxation is a lower bound, so equality and optimality follow.
annotation.complexity: Official variant: O(n^4) time and O(n^2) memory, with O(n^2) output swaps. Hungarian/Kuhn-Munkres plus an O(n)-per-swap reconstruction gives O(n^3) time and O(n^2) memory.
tricks: Interpret the same permutation swap as exchanging either x-coordinates or y-coordinates of labeled planar points.
Temporarily relax paired coordinate exchanges into independent point movement, producing a Manhattan assignment lower bound divided by two.
Choose the smallest unmatched target coordinate; a pigeonhole argument forces a crossing partner that lets both labels move monotonically toward their targets.
The flow or Hungarian edge weights may use the undivided Manhattan distance because the common factor 1/2 does not change the chosen matching.
review_risks: The factor of two is easy to state incorrectly: the raw perfect-matching objective is twice the optimal swap cost, although implementations need not divide edge weights because only the matching is used.
The y-phase targets are q[a_i], not q[i], and swaps must be output using the labels' current x-coordinates after the x-phase.
The official tutorial only sketches the O(n^3) KM/reconstruction optimization; that alternative's exact data-structure bookkeeping was not cross-checked against a separate accepted implementation.

solution_variants.name: min-cost-flow-coordinate-uncrossing
solution_variants.summary: Build a complete bipartite unit-capacity network from initial points (i,p_i) to target points (j,q_j), with edge cost |i-j|+|p_i-q_j|, and recover a minimum perfect matching by min-cost max-flow. Track each matched label's current and target coordinates; repeatedly scan for a straddling pair to fix all x-coordinates and then all y-coordinates, outputting the corresponding swaps.
solution_variants.complexity: O(n^4) time, O(n^2) memory, and O(n^2) output operations.

solution_variants.name: hungarian-optimized-uncrossing
solution_variants.summary: Solve the same complete minimum-cost assignment with the Hungarian/Kuhn-Munkres algorithm in O(n^3). In each coordinate phase maintain or find the mismatched label with smallest target and scan O(n) labels for a straddling partner; since each swap strictly decreases the integral L1 potential and there are O(n^2) swaps, reconstruction is O(n^3). The official tutorial states this optimization but does not spell out all bookkeeping details.
solution_variants.complexity: O(n^3) time, O(n^2) memory, and O(n^2) output operations.

tags.description: Find a perfect matching minimizing the sum of edge costs, usually in a weighted bipartite assignment graph, via min-cost flow or the Hungarian/Kuhn-Munkres algorithm.
tags.created_reason: The existing graph/matching tag does not distinguish weighted minimum-cost assignment from cardinality matching; here the minimum-cost perfect matching is the entire optimization core. Main-agent taxonomy review is required before accepting this candidate.
tags.evidence: The optimal value is one half of the minimum total Manhattan cost of a perfect matching from the n initial points (i,p_i) to the n target points (j,q_j).

tags.description: Send a required amount of flow through a capacitated network while minimizing total edge cost; includes min-cost max-flow used for weighted assignment and transportation models.
tags.created_reason: The existing graph/flow tag does not distinguish ordinary maximum flow from cost-optimizing flow. The official primary implementation uses min-cost flow to compute the weighted perfect matching. Main-agent taxonomy review is required before accepting this candidate.
tags.evidence: The official implementation sends n unit flows through a source-initial-target-sink network whose initial-to-target edge cost is the Manhattan assignment cost.
The decisive reduction embeds permutation entries as planar points (i,p_i) and expresses the relaxed movement objective as Manhattan distance between initial and target points.

The solution drops the requirement that two points move simultaneously, solves the independent-movement assignment relaxation, and then proves by coordinate uncrossing that its lower bound is attainable by legal swaps.
A numeric optimum is insufficient: the algorithm must explicitly realize the chosen matching as legal swaps, first fixing x-coordinates and then y-coordinates without exceeding the matching bound.
Each reconstruction phase greedily selects the mismatched label with smallest target coordinate and swaps it with a straddling partner so both distances decrease monotonically.
The assignment network is bipartite between initial permutation points and target permutation points, with every cross edge carrying a Manhattan cost.
